question: Anticipatory Counseling Regarding Ovarian-Factor Fertility Decline
answer: Clinical Guidance Committee Statement Anticipatory Counseling Regarding Ovarian-Factor Fertility Decline Anticipatory Counseling Regarding Ovarian-Factor Fertility Decline Committee Statement CS Number 22 November 2025 Jump to Jump to Close Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions Background Counseling Non–Age-Related Factors Affecting Ovarian Function Conclusion Resources Conflict of Interest Statement Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms Number 22 (Replaces Committee Opinion No. 589, March 2014) This Committee Statement was developed by the American College of Obstetricians & Gynecologists’ Committee on Clinical Consensus–Gynecology in collaboration with Nancy Sokkary, MD, and Danielle Carter, MD. ABSTRACT: Fertility, or the capacity to produce a child, declines with age as the ovaries undergo a natural decrease in follicle count related to oocyte atresia. However, patients significantly overestimate the likelihood of pregnancy at all ages. It is important that health care professionals educate patients regarding the natural decline in ovarian reserve and quality of egg follicles throughout their reproductive lifespans. Patients who are interested in becoming pregnant also should be educated on non–age-related factors that may accelerate ovarian decline to optimize and more completely understand their reproductive health and family-building opportunities. Despite the existence of resources on ovarian decline and non–age-related factors affecting fertility, more evidence-based resources on ovarian decline are needed for both patients and health care professionals. Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions On the basis of the principles outlined in this Committee Statement, the American College of Obstetricians & Gynecologists (ACOG) makes the following recommendations and conclusions: Obstetrician–gynecologists are encouraged to counsel their patients about ovarian decline as part of routine care, with open communication that honors patient concerns and family-building goals. Obstetrician–gynecologists should advocate for greater, accessible, evidence-based resources on ovarian decline for both health care professionals and patients. It is important that obstetrician–gynecologists and other reproductive health care professionals are aware of the variety of non–age-related factors that may contribute to ovarian decline as they counsel their patients. Background Fertility, or the capacity to produce a child, declines with age due to both quality and quantity of oocytes. Ovaries undergo a natural decrease in follicle count related to oocyte atresia. The maximum number of oocytes is 6–7 million at 20 weeks of gestation in the female fetus. The number of oocytes decreases to approximately 1–2 million at birth; 300,000–500,000 at puberty; 25,000 at age 37 years; and 1,000 at age 51 years, the average age of menopause in the United States 1 2 3 . Ovarian reserve refers to the number of oocytes within the ovaries, which can be measured, although to an imperfect extent. With age, the quality of eggs also declines due to cellular and molecular changes 4 , thus increasing rates of aneuploidy and spontaneous abortion and consequently affecting an individual’s ability to become pregnant 5 . There is not, however, a standardized method to measure the quality of eggs. Additionally, there are several factors that can accelerate the decline of ovarian function outside of chronologic aging. The majority of understanding of natural fertility rates comes from historical data Figure 1 . A study from 1982 evaluating success with donor sperm insemination demonstrated that the pregnancy rate with up to 12 months of insemination cycles decreased from 74% for individuals younger than age 31 years, to 62% for those aged 31–35 years, and 54% for those older than age 35 years. The pregnancy rate per cycle was noted to be higher for women younger than age 35 years compared with those older than 35 years 6 . Fig. 1. Marital fertility rates by 5-year age groups. The ten populations (in descending order at age 20–24 years) are Hutterites, marriages in 1921–1930 (:); Geneva bourgeoisie, husbands born in 1600–1649 (n); Canada, marriages in 1700–1730 (d); Normandy marriages in 1760–90 (B); Hutterites, marriages before 1921 (N); Tunis, marriages of Europeans 1840–1859 (Δ); Normandy, marriages in 1674–1742 (d); Norway, marriages in 1874–1876 (N); Iran, village marriages in 1940–1950 (:); Geneva bourgeoisie, husbands born before 1600 (B). From Menken J, Trussell J, Larsen U. Age and fertility. Science 1986;233:1389–94. Reprinted with permission from AAAS. The American Society for Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) defines infertility as the inability to achieve a successful pregnancy based on the combination of an individual's medical, sexual, and reproductive history, among other factors, such that they require medical intervention to achieve a successful pregnancy 7 . Because of the age-related decrease in fertility and increase in pregnancy loss, the ASRM recommends consultation with a reproductive specialist in patients having regular, unprotected intercourse and without any known etiology for either partner suggestive of impaired reproductive ability when the female partner is aged 8 : 35 years or younger: after 1 year of unsuccessfully attempting to conceive. 36–40 years: after 6 months of unsuccessfully attempting to conceive. Older than 40 years: immediately. For individuals planning to delay childbearing, referral to a reproductive endocrinologist still should be considered. Evaluating egg quantity, such as through measurement of anti-müllerian hormone (AMH), may be a useful, albeit imperfect, predictor of ovarian reserve 9 . Although advances in assisted reproductive technology (ART), including oocyte cryopreservation, have expanded options for family building and have changed approaches to fertility, not all patients have equal access to, are good candidates for, or are aware of the true efficacy of ART. Many patients seeking ART have a false sense of security in the ability of ART to compensate for natural fertility decline 10 . Multiple studies indicate that patients significantly overestimate the likelihood of pregnancy at all ages and are not aware of the rate of fertility decline with age 10 . In a focus group of men and women who self-identified as having low to average levels of knowledge about fertility, most overestimated the reproductive lifespan and fertile window 11 . Another cross-sectional study of women aged 18–29 years demonstrated that, although 44% of participants had discussed fertility-related topics with their health care professionals, only 22% could identify the age of marked fertility decline, the fertile period, and that the ovaries did not continuously produce new eggs 12 . Although fertility awareness generally increases with education level 13 , the overall limited awareness of reproductive aging persisted among undergraduate students, graduate students, and even medical residents 14 15 16 . In fact, obstetrics and gynecology residents were no more likely to answer fertility or oocyte-preservation knowledge questions correctly compared with residents in other specialties 16 . In the aforementioned study of graduate students, more than 50% overestimated in vitro fertilization success rates 15 . As such, educating patients regarding a natural decline in ovarian reserve and the quantity and quality of follicles is important. Individuals with ovaries who may be interested in pregnancy also should be educated on non–age-related factors (discussed later in this document) that may accelerate ovarian decline to optimize and more completely understand their reproductive health and family-building opportunities. It is equally important for health care professionals to counsel their patients on their fertility throughout their reproductive lifespans. At the same time, it is important that reproductive health care professionals acknowledge that not all patients may desire pregnancy or be interested in family building and adjust their counseling as appropriate. Presence of leiomyomas, history of tubal disease or pelvic infection, and endometriosis all can affect fertility but are beyond the scope of this document. Information on the effects of these conditions on fertility may be found in the ASRM Committee Opinion, “Fertility Evaluation of Infertile Women” (https://www.asrm.org/practice-guidance/practice-committee-documents/fertility-evaluation-of-infertile-women-a-committee-opinion-2021/) . This Committee Statement has been updated from Committee Opinion No. 589, Female Age-Related Fertility Decline . This revision includes more up-to-date data, expands on factors that may accelerate a decline in ovarian reserve and negatively affect egg quality, emphasizes inclusivity of family building to all patients interested in pursuing pregnancy, and focuses on the importance of counseling. This document serves as a resource for reproductive health professionals to counsel their patients on fertility decline due to ovarian aging and other factors that may contribute to ovarian decline. Counseling Current Trends in Counseling Obstetrician–gynecologists are encouraged to counsel their patients about ovarian decline as part of routine care, with open communication that honors patient concerns and family-building goals . A significant amount of literature exists for health care professionals regarding natural fertility and prepregnancy counseling, as well as specific counseling surrounding ART and oocyte preservation 8 17 18 . However, there is a paucity of literature and resources, both clinical and patient facing, regarding the most effective methods and timing for counseling patients about their own fertility decline 19 . In a cross-sectional study of physicians by ACOG, only 27.6% of respondents stated that they frequently counsel women aged 18–34 years regarding reproductive aging. That number increased to 75.8% in women aged 35–44 years, even though 82.8% of respondents identified that these conversations were important to have and to have earlier in the reproductive lifespan, especially as a growing percentage of the population is choosing to delay childbearing 20 . According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, the average age of the birthing parent for their first child was 27.5 years in 2023, up from 25.4 years in 2010 21 . Health care professionals also should consider the diversity of family-building preferences, including patients who may not desire pregnancy or children. Same-sex couples and gender-diverse individuals inherently have the same ovarian decline with age but may have additional risk factors or be candidates for ART. From a patient perspective, there is agreement that counseling can come from obstetrician–gynecologists or primary care clinicians 11 . Fertility awareness is greater in patients who have sought medical attention for fertility concerns from a primary care clinician or obstetrician–gynecologist already, suggesting that this conversation is often patient initiated and, therefore, delayed, with 52.9% of patients experiencing 1 year of infertility before seeking medical advice 13 . Patient-Forward Approach to Education on Ovarian Decline Obstetrician–gynecologists should advocate for greater, accessible, evidence-based resources on ovarian decline for both health care professionals and patients . Online educational content and social media are important sources of information for patients. These informational outlets have significant potential to improve knowledge about age-related fertility decline and treatments 11 22 , because 73% of adult women in the United States use social media 23 . Further, in a survey of fertility clinic patients, 79.9% felt that their use of social media benefitted their patient experience 23 . However, the quality of this information may be of particular concern for health care professionals, especially with the rise of misinformation on social media. ACOG recognizes the potential harm misinformation online can have on patients and clinicians and has developed a resource page to combat misinformation in obstetric and gynecologic health care (see the Resources section below). A study about fertility education and social media found that only 10% of posts were by physicians and 1% by professional organizations 24 . There also has been an increase in information within advertisements posted by creators with the potential for profit; 72.2% of these videos featured non–evidence-based information 25 . Despite these limitations, social media will remain a popular resource for patients seeking medical information, and physicians have an opportunity to provide widespread, accessible education with an evidence-based social media presence 24 . Women's health and reproductive health organizations, such as ACOG, the ASRM, and the Society for Women's Health Research, have physician and patient-facing resources regarding fertility and ovarian decline, but most are focused on patients who already have reached the age of expected ovarian decline or are not on readily accessible platforms. Health care professionals are therefore encouraged to incorporate conversations and evidence-based, patient-forward resources about reproductive life planning at visits with patients throughout their reproductive lifespans, not only when concerns surrounding fertility arise, whether initiated by the patient or the physician. A list of known, accessible, and evidence-based resources on fertility and ovarian decline are included in the Resources section. However, more resources on ovarian decline are needed for both patients and health care professionals. As part of educating and counseling patients, health care professionals should have an awareness and knowledge of the several factors outside of age that may contribute to ovarian decline. These factors are discussed in detail in the next section, including specific guidance on counseling where applicable. Non–Age-Related Factors Affecting Ovarian Function It is important that obstetrician–gynecologists and other reproductive health care professionals are aware of the variety of non–age-related factors that may contribute to ovarian decline as they counsel their patients . A diverse array of non–age-related factors, ranging from inherited anomalies to environmental exposures, may affect the ovaries by diverse mechanisms. Some of these genetic, iatrogenic, and environmental factors are discussed in further detail later in the document. However, in many cases, the exact implications of these non–age-related influences are not yet known or have conflicting data in previous studies. Box 1 lists factors that may affect ovarian function, with limited or conflicting data. The number of factors that potentially affect the ovaries and the lack of conclusive data warrant further investigation and a supportive approach to patient counseling. The effects of these factors on pregnancy itself is covered in ACOG Committee Opinion No. 762, Prepregnancy Counseling . Box 1. Factors Possibly Affecting Ovarian Function Autoimmune diseases 26 27 28 29 30 Thyroid disease* Addison’s disease Diabetes* Rheumatoid arthritis† Crohn’s disease† Myasthenia gravis† Systemic lupus erythematosus† Multiple sclerosis Sickle cell anemia† Endometriosis 31 32 33 34 * Testosterone for gendering-affirming care 35 36 † Alcohol consumption †‡ Endocrine Disruptors 37 § Perfluoroalkyl substances 38 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD) 39 Bisphenol-A (BPA) 39 40 Phthalates 41 Parabens * Strong association. † Weak association. ‡ Conflicting evidence. § Available evidence suggests causation and warrants further investigation. Genetic Familial and genetic factors are known to influence the age of menopause, and knowledge regarding genetic effects on ovarian decline specifically has expanded tremendously. Certain genetic disorders, such as fragile X premutation and Turner syndrome, are clearly associated with a decrease in ovarian reserve and with primary ovarian insufficiency 42 . There is a rapidly growing body of evidence regarding additional specific gene mutations that contribute to diminished ovarian reserve, with more than 50 genes implicated in primary ovarian insufficiency and 29% of women with primary ovarian insufficiency having variants in whole-exome sequencing 43 44 45 . A family history of primary ovarian insufficiency, particularly among first-degree relatives such as siblings, poses a significant risk for primary ovarian insufficiency 46 . Routine genetic screening to evaluate for risk of ovarian decline in patients at low to average risk is not recommended. Iatrogenic Several necessary iatrogenic interventions result in diminished ovarian reserve, including pelvic radiation and oophorotoxic chemotherapeutic agents (refer to ACOG Committee Opinion No. 747, Gynecologic Issues in Children and Adolescent Cancer Patients and Survivors ). Unilateral oophorectomy will diminish ovarian reserve, but partial oophorectomy and cystectomy also may have implications, because they can negatively affect the microenvironment and adnexal blood supply 47 . Exposures Substance use and environmental exposures also may affect ovarian function. This area often garners media attention and patient interest, but many potential risk factors are not yet well understood. The Women's Health Initiative Study found an increase in infertility and a younger age of menopause among women who smoked or were exposed to high levels of secondhand smoke compared with women in a control group. This has been a consistent finding 48 . An ASRM Committee Opinion states that extremes of body mass index (BMI), smoking, high caffeine, and mercury exposure all may increase infertility rates, which does not necessarily reflect ovarian reserve alone 8 . Conflicting data exist regarding the effect of alcohol consumption on ovaries and fertility. One systematic review found no association with low AMH levels and alcohol consumption. Most studies included in the review were primarily comparing consumers of alcohol with nonconsumers and did not delineate amount of consumption 49 . A large cross-sectional study found a significantly lower AMH level among women who reported consuming alcohol 48 . The ASRM recommends avoiding more than two drinks per day while attempting to become pregnant and notes limited data regarding moderate alcohol consumption affecting fertility 8 . Endocrine disrupters are synthetic chemicals found in both the environment and common household products 48 . Both mainstream media and scientific journals have increased their focus on endocrine disruptors because of their ubiquity in everyday life and preliminary data suggesting that exposure likely leads to ovarian function decline. Patient concerns should be validated and evolving recommendations shared. A list of common endocrine disruptors can be found in Box 1 , and an overview of pesticides and ways for patients to minimize exposure can be found on the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s website (About Pesticides and Reproductive Health) . This is a rapidly expanding area of research that both clinicians and patients should be encouraged to evaluate continually. Conclusion Reproductive health care professionals are encouraged to counsel their patients regarding ovarian decline and family building throughout their reproductive lifespans. Many individuals do not understand the implications of decreasing ovarian reserve, or they overestimate their ability to become pregnant. Patient education on fertility and non–age-related factors affecting ovarian function may be further complicated or misunderstood due to the rise of medical misinformation on social media. Obstetrician–gynecologists and other reproductive health care professionals, therefore, should not wait to initiate reproductive life–planning discussions until patients raise concerns about their fertility and treatment options. Although there are no specific guidelines addressing counseling on ovarian decline, it should be acknowledged that many factors in addition to age may contribute to an individual’s ovarian function. At present, specific genetic anomalies, environmental factors, medical treatments, and systemic illnesses are known to result in a decrease in ovarian reserve and egg quality. However, there are more factors that are not yet fully understood in terms of their effect on ovarian function. Navigating these risks can be overwhelming for patients and health care professionals. For that reason, it is important to have an open and ongoing dialogue about family building between health care professionals and their patients who are pursuing pregnancy. It is also imperative to continue the study of ovarian decline and create appropriate, evidence-based, and accessible clinician and patient resources. question: Viral Hepatitis in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Clinical Practice Guideline Viral Hepatitis in Pregnancy Viral Hepatitis in Pregnancy Clinical Practice Guideline CPG Number 6 September 2023 Jump to Jump to Close Introduction Summary of Recommendations Methods Clinical Overview Clinical Recommendations and Evidence Summary Appendices Conflict of Interest Statement Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms Purpose: The purpose of this document is to describe the specific types of viral hepatitis, their implications during pregnancy, the risk of perinatal transmission, and issues related to both treatment and prevention of infection. Target Population: Pregnant or postpartum women and individuals who screen positive for viral hepatitis infection. The onset of these conditions may have predated the perinatal period or may have occurred for the first time in pregnancy or the first year postpartum. Methods: This guideline was developed using an a priori protocol in conjunction with a writing team consisting of one specialist in obstetrics and gynecology appointed by the ACOG Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines–Obstetrics and one external subject matter expert. ACOG medical librarians completed a comprehensive literature search for primary literature within Cochrane Library, Cochrane Collaboration Registry of Controlled Trials, EMBASE, PubMed, and MEDLINE. Studies that moved forward to the full-text screening stage were assessed by two authors from the writing team based on standardized inclusion and exclusion criteria. Included studies underwent quality assessment, and a modified GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation) evidence-to-decision framework was applied to interpret and translate the evidence into recommendation statements. Recommendations: This Clinical Practice Guideline includes recommendations on hepatitis B virus and hepatitis C virus screening in pregnancy; prepregnancy, antepartum, intrapartum, and postpartum management for patients with hepatitis B virus infection or hepatitis C virus infection; management of accidental and occupational exposure to hepatitis B virus or hepatitis C virus in pregnant health care workers; and hepatitis A virus and hepatitis B virus vaccination in pregnancy. Recommendations are classified by strength and evidence quality. Ungraded Good Practice Points are included to provide guidance when a formal recommendation could not be made because of inadequate or nonexistent evidence. question: Screening and Diagnosis of Mental Health Conditions During Pregnancy and Postpartum
answer: Clinical Guidance Clinical Practice Guideline Screening and Diagnosis of Mental Health Conditions During Pregnancy and Postpartum Screening and Diagnosis of Mental Health Conditions During Pregnancy and Postpartum Clinical Practice Guideline CPG Number 4 June 2023 Jump to Jump to Close Introduction Summary of Recommendations Methods Clinical Overview Clinical Recommendations and Evidence Summary Appendices Conflict of Interest Statement Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms Purpose: To review evidence on the current understanding of mental health conditions in pregnancy and postpartum, with a focus on mood and anxiety disorders, and to outline guidelines for screening and diagnosis that are consistent with best available scientific evidence. The conditions or symptoms reviewed include depression, anxiety and anxiety-related disorders, bipolar disorder, suicidality, and postpartum psychosis. For information on psychopharmacologic treatment and management, refer to American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) Clinical Practice Guideline Number 5, “Treatment and Management of Mental Health Conditions During Pregnancy and Postpartum” 1 . Target Population: Pregnant or postpartum individuals with mental health conditions. Onset of these conditions may have predated the perinatal period or may have occurred for the first time in pregnancy or the first year postpartum or may have been exacerbated in that time. Methods: This guideline was developed using an a priori protocol in conjunction with a writing team consisting of one specialist in obstetrics and gynecology and one maternal–fetal medicine subspecialist appointed by the ACOG Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines–Obstetrics and two external subject matter experts. ACOG medical librarians completed a comprehensive literature search for primary literature within Cochrane Library, Cochrane Collaboration Registry of Controlled Trials, EMBASE, PubMed, and MEDLINE. Studies that moved forward to the full-text screening stage were assessed by two authors from the writing team based on standardized inclusion and exclusion criteria. Included studies underwent quality assessment, and a modified GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) evidence-to-decision framework was applied to interpret and translate the evidence into recommendation statements. Recommendations: This Clinical Practice Guideline includes recommendations on the screening and diagnosis of perinatal mental health conditions including depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, acute postpartum psychosis, and the symptom of suicidality. Recommendations are classified by strength and evidence quality. Ungraded Good Practice Points are included to provide guidance when a formal recommendation could not be made because of inadequate or nonexistent evidence. question: Intrapartum Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring: Interpretation and Management
answer: Clinical Guidance Clinical Practice Guideline Intrapartum Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring: Interpretation and Management Intrapartum Fetal Heart Rate Monitoring: Interpretation and Management Clinical Practice Guideline CPG Number 10 October 2025 Jump to Jump to Close Introduction Summary of Recommendations Methods Clinical Overview Clinical Recommendations and Evidence Summary Conclusion Appendices Conflict of Interest Statement Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms Purpose: The purpose of this document is to provide an evidence-based framework for the evaluation and management of intrapartum fetal heart rate (FHR) patterns. Target Population: Pregnant individuals in the first or second stage of labor. Methods: This guideline was developed using an a priori protocol in conjunction with a writing team consisting of three maternal–fetal medicine subspecialists and one specialist in obstetrics and gynecology appointed by the American College of Obstetricians & Gynecologists’ (ACOG) Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines–Obstetrics. ACOG medical librarians completed a comprehensive literature search for primary literature within Cochrane Library, Cochrane Collaboration Registry of Controlled Trials, EMBASE, PubMed, and MEDLINE. Studies that moved forward to the full-text screening stage were assessed by the writing team based on standardized inclusion and exclusion criteria. Included studies underwent quality assessment, and a modified GRADE (Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation) evidence-to-decision framework was applied to interpret and translate the evidence into recommendation statements. Recommendations: This Clinical Practice Guideline includes an overview of intrapartum FHR monitoring nomenclature and classification systems and provides recommendations for evaluation and management of intrapartum FHR tracings. question: Bariatric Surgery and Pregnancy

Read terms ABSTRACT: As the rate of obesity increases, it is becoming more common for providers of women’s health care to encounter patients who are either contemplating or have had operative procedures for weight loss, also known as bariatric surgery. The counseling and management of patients who become pregnant after bariatric surgery can be complex. Although pregnancy outcomes generally have been favorable after bariatric surgery, nutritional and surgical complications can occur and some of these complications can result in adverse perinatal outcomes. The purpose of this Practice Bulletin is to provide a summary of the risks of obesity in pregnancy, review the available literature regarding outcomes of pregnancy after bariatric surgery, and provide recommendations for the care of the patient during her pregnancy and delivery after bariatric surgery. question: Cytomegalovirus, Parvovirus B19, Varicella Zoster, and Toxoplasmosis in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Cytomegalovirus, Parvovirus B19, Varicella Zoster, and Toxoplasmosis in Pregnancy Cytomegalovirus, Parvovirus B19, Varicella Zoster, and Toxoplasmosis in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 151 June 2015 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions Proposed Performance Measure Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Among the many physiologic changes that occur during pregnancy, the maternal immune system is altered to dampen the maternal inflammatory response and allow for fetal antigen tolerance 1 2 . Although such immunologic changes diminish the chance of fetal rejection, they potentially increase maternal and fetal vulnerability to certain infectious diseases. Common infections that cause mild-to-moderate disease in healthy adults and children can cause serious maternal and fetal complications if acquired during pregnancy. A unique concern with maternal infection is the potential for mother-to-child transmission or congenital infection. Cytomegalovirus (CMV), parvovirus B19, varicella zoster virus (VZV), and toxoplasmosis are common infections associated with moderate-to-severe fetal and infant complications when acquired congenitally. The purpose of this document is to update the current understanding of these infections, including their clinical presentations; their modes and risks of perinatal transmission; and their maternal, fetal, and infant effects, and to offer guidelines for preventing and managing these infections during pregnancy. question: Prenatal Diagnostic Testing for Genetic Disorders
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Prenatal Diagnostic Testing for Genetic Disorders Prenatal Diagnostic Testing for Genetic Disorders Practice Bulletin PB Number 162 May 2016 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions For More Information Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Prenatal genetic diagnostic testing is intended to determine, with as much certainty as possible, whether a specific genetic disorder or condition is present in the fetus. In contrast, prenatal genetic screening is designed to assess whether a patient is at increased risk of having a fetus affected by a genetic disorder. Originally, prenatal genetic testing focused primarily on Down syndrome (trisomy 21), but now it is able to detect a broad range of genetic disorders. Although it is necessary to perform amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling (CVS) to definitively diagnose most genetic disorders, in some circumstances, fetal imaging with ultrasonography, echocardiography, or magnetic resonance imaging may be diagnostic of a particular structural fetal abnormality that is suggestive of an underlying genetic condition. The objective of prenatal genetic testing is to detect health problems that could affect the woman, fetus, or newborn and provide the patient and her obstetrician–gynecologist or other obstetric care provider with enough information to allow a fully informed decision about pregnancy management. Prenatal genetic testing cannot identify all abnormalities or problems in a fetus, and any testing should be focused on the individual patient’s risks, reproductive goals, and preferences. It is important that patients understand the benefits and limitations of all prenatal screening and diagnostic testing, including the conditions for which tests are available and the conditions that will not be detected by testing. It also is important that patients realize that there is a broad range of clinical presentations, or phenotypes, for many genetic disorders and that results of genetic testing cannot predict all outcomes. Prenatal genetic testing has many benefits, including reassuring patients when results are normal, identifying disorders for which prenatal treatment may provide benefit, optimizing neonatal outcomes by ensuring the appropriate location for delivery and the necessary personnel to care for affected infants, and allowing the opportunity for pregnancy termination. The purpose of this Practice Bulletin is to review the current status of prenatal genetic diagnostic testing and the evidence supporting its use. For information regarding screening for fetal aneuploidy, refer to Practice Bulletin No. 163, Screening for Fetal Aneuploidy. question: Use of Prophylactic Antibiotics in Labor and Delivery
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Use of Prophylactic Antibiotics in Labor and Delivery Use of Prophylactic Antibiotics in Labor and Delivery Practice Bulletin PB Number 199 September 2018 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: The use of antibiotics to prevent infections during the antepartum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods is different than the use of antibiotics to treat established infections. For many years, the use of prophylactic antibiotics was thought to have few adverse consequences. Concerns about the emergence of resistant strains of common bacteria, in addition to the emergence of strains with increased virulence, have resulted in increased scrutiny of the use of antibiotics, particularly in the hospital setting. Awareness of the potential adverse effects of resistant bacterial infections on neonates has been growing. Attention has been focused on the effect of mode of delivery or early antibiotic exposure on the neonatal oral and gut microbiome, which is essential for immune development. Finally, cost is a consideration in the use and choice of prophylactic agents. The purpose of this Practice Bulletin is to present a review of clinical situations in which prophylactic antibiotics are frequently prescribed and to weigh the evidence that supports the use of antibiotics in these scenarios. This Practice Bulletin is updated to reflect a limited change to clarify and provide additional information on recommendations from recent consensus guidelines for antimicrobial prophylaxis in surgery and the prevention of surgical site infection. The following practices related to cesarean delivery include preoperative skin and vaginal cleansing, weight-based dosage for cefazolin antibiotic prophylaxis, the addition of adjunctive azithromycin antibiotic prophylaxis, and antibiotic selection and dosage for women with a penicillin allergy. question: Inherited Thrombophilias in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Inherited Thrombophilias in Pregnancy Inherited Thrombophilias in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 197 July 2018 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations For More Information Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Inherited thrombophilias are associated with an increased risk of venous thromboembolism and have been linked to adverse outcomes in pregnancy. However, there is limited evidence to guide screening for and management of these conditions in pregnancy. The purpose of this document is to review common thrombophilias and their association with maternal venous thromboembolism risk and adverse pregnancy outcomes, indications for screening to detect these conditions, and management options in pregnancy. This Practice Bulletin has been revised to provide additional information on recommendations for candidates for thrombophilia evaluation, updated consensus guidelines regarding the need for prophylaxis in women with an inherited thrombophilia during pregnancy and the postpartum period, and discussion of new published consensus guidelines from the Society for Obstetric Anesthesia and Perinatology addressing thromboprophylaxis and neuraxial anesthetic considerations in the obstetric population. question: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 193 March 2018 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Ectopic pregnancy is defined as a pregnancy that occurs outside of the uterine cavity. The most common site of ectopic pregnancy is the fallopian tube. Most cases of tubal ectopic pregnancy that are detected early can be treated successfully either with minimally invasive surgery or with medical management using methotrexate. However, tubal ectopic pregnancy in an unstable patient is a medical emergency that requires prompt surgical intervention. The purpose of this document is to review information on the current understanding of tubal ectopic pregnancy and to provide guidelines for timely diagnosis and management that are consistent with the best available scientific evidence. question: Critical Care in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Critical Care in Pregnancy Critical Care in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 211 May 2019 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Recommendations and Conclusions For More Information Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Critical care in pregnancy relies predominantly on recommendations from nonpregnant adult critical care with only limited research available for obstetric critical care specifically. The purpose of this document is to review available evidence, propose strategies for obstetric-related critical care, and highlight the need for additional research. Much of the review will, of necessity, focus on general principles of critical care, extrapolating when possible to critical care in pregnancy and the puerperium. This Practice Bulletin is updated to include information about unique issues to pregnancy when conditions such as sepsis or acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) are encountered and the obstetrician's role in the management of the critically ill pregnant woman, which is dependent upon the care setting and the intensive care unit (ICU) model used. The role of the tele-intensive care unit in the care of critically ill pregnant women also is explored. Log in to read more This content is only available to members and subscribers. question: Vaginal Birth After Cesarean Delivery
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Vaginal Birth After Cesarean Delivery Vaginal Birth After Cesarean Delivery Practice Bulletin PB Number 205 February 2019 Jump to Jump to Close Background Evaluating the Evidence Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions For More Information Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Trial of labor after cesarean delivery (TOLAC) refers to a planned attempt to deliver vaginally by a woman who has had a previous cesarean delivery, regardless of the outcome. This method provides women who desire a vaginal delivery the possibility of achieving that goal—a vaginal birth after cesarean delivery (VBAC). In addition to fulfilling a patient’s preference for vaginal delivery, at an individual level, VBAC is associated with decreased maternal morbidity and a decreased risk of complications in future pregnancies as well as a decrease in the overall cesarean delivery rate at the population level 1 2 3 . However, although TOLAC is appropriate for many women, several factors increase the likelihood of a failed trial of labor, which in turn is associated with increased maternal and perinatal morbidity when compared with a successful trial of labor (ie, VBAC) and elective repeat cesarean delivery 4 5 6 . Therefore, assessing the likelihood of VBAC as well as the individual risks is important when determining who is an appropriate candidate for TOLAC. Thus, the purpose of this document is to review the risks and benefits of TOLAC in various clinical situations and to provide practical guidelines for counseling and management of patients who will attempt to give birth vaginally after a previous cesarean delivery. question: Management of Genital Herpes in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Management of Genital Herpes in Pregnancy Management of Genital Herpes in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 220 May 2020 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Genital herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection during pregnancy poses a risk to the developing fetus and newborn. Genital herpes is common in the United States. Among 14- to 49-year-old females, the prevalence of HSV-2 infection is 15.9%. However, the prevalence of genital herpes infection is higher than that because genital herpes is also caused by HSV-1 1 . Because many women of childbearing age are infected or will be infected with HSV, the risk of maternal transmission of this virus to the fetus or newborn is a major health concern. This document has been revised to include that for women with a primary or nonprimary first-episode genital HSV infection during the third trimester of pregnancy, cesarean delivery may be offered due to the possibility of prolonged viral shedding. question: Operative Vaginal Birth
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Operative Vaginal Birth Operative Vaginal Birth Practice Bulletin PB Number 219 April 2020 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Issues Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Recommendations and Conclusions Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Despite significant changes in management of labor and delivery over the past few decades, operative vaginal birth remains an important component of modern labor management, accounting for 3.3% of all deliveries in 2013 1 . Use of obstetric forceps or vacuum extractor requires that an obstetrician or other obstetric care provider be familiar with the proper use of the instruments and the risks involved. The purpose of this document is to provide a review of the current evidence regarding the benefits and risks of operative vaginal birth. question: Anemia in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Anemia in Pregnancy Anemia in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 233 August 2021 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Anemia, the most common hematologic abnormality, is a reduction in the concentration of erythrocytes or hemoglobin in blood. The two most common causes of anemia in pregnancy and the puerperium are iron deficiency and acute blood loss. Iron requirements increase during pregnancy, and a failure to maintain sufficient levels of iron may result in adverse maternal–fetal consequences. The purpose of this document is to provide a brief overview of the causes of anemia in pregnancy, review iron requirements, and provide recommendations for screening and clinical management of anemia during pregnancy. question: Obesity in Pregnancy
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Obesity in Pregnancy Obesity in Pregnancy Practice Bulletin PB Number 230 June 2021 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Obstetrician–gynecologists are the leading experts in the health care of women, and obesity is the most common medical condition in women of reproductive age. Obesity in women is such a common condition that the implications relative to pregnancy often are unrecognized, overlooked, or ignored because of the lack of specific evidence-based treatment options. The management of obesity requires long-term approaches ranging from population-based public health and economic initiatives to individual nutritional, behavioral, or surgical interventions. Therefore, an understanding of the management of obesity during pregnancy is essential, and management should begin before pregnancy and continue through the postpartum period. Although the care of the obese woman during pregnancy requires the involvement of the obstetrician or other obstetric care professional , additional health care professionals, such as nutritionists, can offer specific expertise related to management depending on the comfort level of the obstetric care professional . The purpose of this Practice Bulletin is to offer an integrated approach to the management of obesity in women of reproductive age who are planning a pregnancy. question: Fetal Growth Restriction
answer: Clinical Guidance Practice Bulletin Fetal Growth Restriction Fetal Growth Restriction Practice Bulletin PB Number 227 February 2021 Jump to Jump to Close Background Clinical Considerations and Recommendations Summary of Recommendations and Conclusions Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms ABSTRACT: Fetal growth restriction, also known as intrauterine growth restriction, is a common complication of pregnancy that has been associated with a variety of adverse perinatal outcomes. There is a lack of consensus regarding terminology, etiology, and diagnostic criteria for fetal growth restriction, with uncertainty surrounding the optimal management and timing of delivery for the growth-restricted fetus. An additional challenge is the difficulty in differentiating between the fetus that is constitutionally small and fulfilling its growth potential and the small fetus that is not fulfilling its growth potential because of an underlying pathologic condition. The purpose of this document is to review the topic of fetal growth restriction with a focus on terminology, etiology, diagnostic and surveillance tools, and guidance for management and timing of delivery. question: Neonatal Encephalopathy and Neurologic Outcome
answer: Clinical Guidance Task Force Report Neonatal Encephalopathy and Neurologic Outcome Neonatal Encephalopathy and Neurologic Outcome Task Force Report TF Jump to Jump to Close Search page Search Page Close Resources Resources Close Share Facebook Bluesky LinkedIn Email Print By reading this page you agree to ACOG's Terms and Conditions. Read terms (Reaffirmed 2019) In the first edition of this report, the Task Force on Neonatal Encephalopathy and Cerebral Palsy outlined criteria deemed essential to establish a causal link between intrapartum hypoxic events and cerebral palsy. It is now known that there are multiple potential causal pathways that lead to cerebral palsy in term infants, and the signs and symptoms of neonatal encephalopathy may range from mild to severe, depending on the nature and timing of the brain injury. Thus, for the current edition, the Task Force on Neonatal Encephalopathy determined that a broader perspective may be more fruitful. Included in the new materials is expanded information on acid-base parameters of umbilical cord blood and the inappropriate use of the terms fetal distress and birth asphyxia. question: Maltreatment, Violence, and Self-Injury | Child Development
answer: Maltreatment, Violence, and Self-Injury Jan. 28, 2026 At a glance Although children with disabilities are no more aggressive than other children, they may have an increased chance of being victims of bullying, abuse, and neglect. Childhood maltreatment Parents can more easily become stressed with the demands placed on them by parenting a child with a disability. Kids with behavior problems, such as attention-deficit / hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) or other conduct problems, may be more likely to experience physical abuse because parents can become frustrated by the child's difficult behavior and respond harshly. Kids who are less able to do things independently rely more on adults for their care. These children may be more likely to be sexually abused or neglected by adults. Abusers may take advantage of kids who have problems speaking or hearing or who don't understand social situations very well. These children may be more likely to experience sexual abuse. Keep Reading Child Abuse and Neglect Prevention Youth violence and aggression Most children with developmental disabilities are not any more violent or aggressive than other children. However, some children may feel a lot of frustration related to their developmental disability. This frustration is sometimes shown through aggression or even self-harming behaviors, such as banging their head or cutting their skin. Other children have conditions that are more directly connected to aggressive behavior. For example, children with oppositional defiant disorder are often annoyed and angry, and they argue with adults in order to gain control. There are many reasons children with developmental disabilities may have aggression problems. It is important to remember that everyone has times when they get frustrated or angry, and children should be taught that frustration is normal. It is best to try to understand the reasons behind the aggression and violence. Knowing this will help parents and health professionals work toward reducing the problems; teaching the child ways to cope with frustration should be part of this plan. Keep Reading Preventing Youth Violence Bullying Children with disabilities may have low self-esteem or feel depressed, lonely, or anxious because of their disability, and bullying may make this even worse. Bullying can cause serious, lasting problems not only for children who are bullied but also for children who bully and those who witness bullying. Children do not always know when they are bullied. They might feel bad but don't know how to talk about it. Children with disabilities that affect how they think, learn, or interact with others might need a very detailed explanation about how to recognize bullying when it happens to themselves or others. Keep Reading Tourette Syndrome and Bullying Self-injury Children and youth with developmental disabilities, such as autism and intellectual disability, are more likely to engage in other forms of self-injury than children without these disabilities. 1 2 3 Youth with depression, anxiety disorder, and conduct disorder have a higher chance of self-violence, including suicide, than children without these disorders. 4 It is important to understand the reasons for self-directed violence and other forms of self-injury among children with disabilities in order to find the best treatment. It is also important to coordinate care given by family, school, and healthcare providers. Find help: SAMHSA's 988 Suicide and Crisis Helpline If you or someone you know is struggling or in crisis, help is available. Call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org . On This Page Childhood maltreatment Youth violence and aggression Bullying Self-injury Jan. 28, 2026 Sources Print Share Facebook LinkedIn Twitter Syndicate Content Source: National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities References Schroeder, S.R., Oster-Granite, M.L., and Thompson, T. (Eds.) (2002). Self-injurious behavior: Gene-brain-behavior relationships. Washington, D.C.: American Psychological Association. Richards, C., Oliver, C., Nelson, L., & Moss, J. (2012). Self-injurious behaviour in individuals with autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability . Journal of Intellectual Disabilities Research, 56, 476-489. question: Positive Parenting Tips: Preschoolers (3–5 years old) | Child Development
answer: Positive Parenting Tips: Preschoolers (3–5 years old) Feb. 20, 2026 Español Key points As a parent you give your children a good start in life—you nurture, protect, and guide them. Learn about developmental milestones, including emotional and social development, for preschoolers from 3 to 5 years old. There are many things you can do to help your child stay safe and healthy. Developmental milestones Skills such as naming colors, showing affection, and hopping on one foot are called developmental milestones. Most children achieve these milestones by a certain age. Children reach milestones in how they play, learn, speak, behave, and move. As children grow into early childhood, their world will open up as they explore and ask about the things around them. They will become more independent and begin to focus more on adults and children outside of the family. Their interactions with those around them will help to shape their personality and their own ways of thinking and moving. During this stage, children should be able to help dress and undress themselves, recall part of a story, and sing songs. Other examples of activities at this stage include playing with other children, riding a tricycle, and using safety scissors. Positive parenting tips Encourage your child to play with other children to help them learn the value of sharing and friendship. Following are some of the things you, as a parent, can do to help your preschooler during this time: Continue to read to your child. Nurture their love for books by taking them to the library or bookstore. Let your child help with simple chores. Be clear and consistent when disciplining your child. Explain and show the behavior that you expect from them. Whenever you tell them no, follow up with what they should be doing instead. Help your child develop good language skills by speaking to them in complete sentences and using "grown up" words. Help them to use the correct words and phrases. Help your child through the steps to solve problems when they are upset. Give your child a limited number of simple choices (for example, deciding what to wear, when to play, and what to eat for snack). Child safety first As your child becomes more independent, it is important that you and your child are aware of ways to stay safe. Here are a few tips to protect your child: Tell your child why it is important to stay out of traffic. Tell them not to play in the street or run after stray balls. Be cautious when letting your child ride their tricycle. Keep them on the sidewalk and away from the street and always have them wear a helmet. Check outdoor playground equipment. Make sure there are no loose parts or sharp edges. Watch your child at all times, especially when they are playing outside. Be safe in the water. Teach your child to swim, but watch them at all times when they are in or around any body of water (this includes kiddie pools). Teach your child how to be safe around strangers. Car seat recommendations for children‎‎ Use a forward-facing car seat with a harness until your child reaches the top height or weight limit allowed by the car seat's manufacturer. Then it will be time to switch to a booster seat, but still in the back seat of the vehicle. Learn more: Car seat recommendations for children (National Highway Traffic Safety Administration) Healthy bodies Here are a few tips to help keep your growing child healthy: Eat meals with your child whenever possible. Let your child see you enjoying fruits, vegetables, and whole grains at meals and snacks. Your child should eat and drink only a limited amount of food and beverages that contain added sugars, solid fats, or salt. Provide your child with age-appropriate play equipment, like balls and plastic bats, but let your preschooler choose what to play. This makes moving and being active fun for your preschooler. Make sure your child gets the recommended amount of sleep each night: 10–13 hours per 24 hours (including naps) for preschoolers 3–5 years old. Screen time and preschoolers Keep television sets out of your child's bedroom. Set limits for screen time for your child to no more than 1 hour per day of quality programming, at home, school, or afterschool care and develop a media use plan for your family. Learn more: Media use plan (Healthy Children) For more information CDC's "Learn the Signs. Act Early." Program has more details on how to track your child's developmental milestones, and what to do if you're concerned about your child's development. CDC's Parent Information (Children 4–11 years) has information to help you guide your child in leading a healthier life. CDC's Essentials for Parenting Toddlers and Preschoolers has ways you can help build a safe, stable, and nurturing relationship with your child. CDC's Healthy Weight Information has tips for parents – Ideas to help children maintain a healthy weight. CDC's Youth Physical Activity Guidelines has information on how to help children be active and play. CDC's Information on Vaccinations View the immunization schedule for infants and children and find out if your child's vaccinations are up to date. The U.S. Department of Agriculture provides information on health and nutrition: My Plate for Preschoolers AAP's Healthy Children website provides information on feeding, nutrition, and fitness for all developmental stages from infancy to young adulthood. Just in Time Parenting (JITP) has quality, research-based information to families at the time it can be most useful. Healthy Kids Healthy Future has information on physical activity for young children and ways to keep them moving. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) has information on safety recalls and safety tips for children riding in motor vehicles, walking, biking, playing outside, waiting at school bus stops, and more. On This Page Developmental milestones Positive parenting tips Child safety first Healthy bodies For more information Feb. 20, 2026 Sources Print Share Facebook LinkedIn Twitter Syndicate Content Source: National Center on Birth Defects and Developmental Disabilities
summary: Positive parenting tips and resources for preschoolers (3–5 years old)
